title: Mean and standard deviation of difference of sample proportions
description: Practice calculating the mean and standard deviation of sampling distributions for differences in sample proportions, and use the large count condition to determine when these sampling distributions are approximately normal.
topic_path: Sampling distributions for differences in sample proportions
Mean and standard deviation of difference of sample proportions

assessment_items.question_content:
Suppose that $60\%$ of adults in District A support a new ballot measure, while $45\%$ of adults in District B support the same measure. Pollsters take an SRS of $200$ adults from District A and a separate SRS of $100$ adults from District B to see the difference between the sample proportions $(\hat{p}_\text{A}-\hat{p}_\text{B})$.

**What are the mean and standard deviation of the sampling distribution of $\hat{p}_\text{A}-\hat{p}_\text{B}$?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Formulas

The mean of the sampling distribution of $\hat{p}_1-\hat{p}_2$ is the difference of the population proportions:

$\mu_{\hat{p}_1-\hat{p}_2}=p_1-p_2$

The standard deviation of $\hat{p}_1-\hat{p}_2$ is: 

$\sigma_{\hat{p}_1-\hat{p}_2}=\sqrt{\dfrac{p_1\left(1-p_1\right)}{n_1}+\dfrac{p_2\left(1-p_2\right)}{n_2}}$

(where $n_1$ and $n_2$ are the sizes of each sample).

###Identifying values

"...$\blueE{60\%}$ of adults in District A support a new ballot measure, while $\purpleD{45\%}$ of adults in District B support the same measure. Pollsters take an SRS of $\maroonE{200}$ adults from District A and a separate SRS of $\goldE{100}$ adults from District B..."

District | Population proportion | Sample size
:-: | :-: | :-:
A | $\blueE{p_\text{A}}=\blueE{0.60}$ | $\maroonE{n_\text{A}}=\maroonE{200}$
B | $\purpleD{p_\text{B}}=\purpleD{0.45}$ | $\goldE{n_\text{B}}=\goldE{100}$

assessment_items.question_content:
A geology teacher takes her class on an annual field trip to analyze rock samples. They use randomized sets of coordinates to select samples from two regions.

Suppose they examine a sample of $70$ rocks from region A, which is composed of $15\%$ igneous rock. Then they examine a sample of $60$ rocks from region B, which is composed of $20\%$ igneous rock. Then they look at the difference between the sample proportions $(\hat{p}_\text{A}-\hat{p}_\text{B})$.

**What are the mean and standard deviation of the sampling distribution of $\hat{p}_\text{A}-\hat{p}_\text{B}$?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Formulas

The mean of the sampling distribution of $\hat{p}_1-\hat{p}_2$ is the difference of the population proportions:

$\mu_{\hat{p}_1-\hat{p}_2}=p_1-p_2$

The standard deviation of $\hat{p}_1-\hat{p}_2$ is: 

$\sigma_{\hat{p}_1-\hat{p}_2}=\sqrt{\dfrac{p_1\left(1-p_1\right)}{n_1}+\dfrac{p_2\left(1-p_2\right)}{n_2}}$

(where $n_1$ and $n_2$ are the sizes of each sample).

###Identifying values

"...they examine a sample of $\maroonE{70}$ rocks from region A, which is composed of $\blueE{15\%}$ igneous rock. Then they examine a sample of $\goldE{60}$ rocks from region B, which is composed of $\purpleD{20\%}$ igneous rock...."

Region | Population proportion | Sample size
:-: | :-: | :-:
A | $\blueE{p_\text{A}}=\blueE{0.15}$ | $\maroonE{n_\text{A}}=\maroonE{70}$
B | $\purpleD{p_\text{B}}=\purpleD{0.2}$ | $\goldE{n_\text{B}}=\goldE{60}$

assessment_items.question_content:
In 2006, $95\%$ of new cars in the US came with a spare tire. In 2017, $72\%$ came with a spare tire.

Suppose a researcher takes separate random samples of $250$ cars made in each year, then looks at the difference between the sample proportions $\hat{p}_{2006}-\hat{p}_{2017}$ of cars with spare tires in each sample.

**What are the mean and standard deviation of the sampling distribution of $\hat{p}_{2006}-\hat{p}_{2017}$?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Formulas

The mean of the sampling distribution of $\hat{p}_1-\hat{p}_2$ is the difference of the population proportions:

$\mu_{\hat{p}_1-\hat{p}_2}=p_1-p_2$

The standard deviation of $\hat{p}_1-\hat{p}_2$ is: 

$\sigma_{\hat{p}_1-\hat{p}_2}=\sqrt{\dfrac{p_1\left(1-p_1\right)}{n_1}+\dfrac{p_2\left(1-p_2\right)}{n_2}}$

(where $n_1$ and $n_2$ are the sizes of each sample).

###Identifying values

"...In 2006, $\blueE{95\%}$ of new cars in the US came with a spare tire. In 2017, $\purpleD{72\%}$ came with a spare tire....a researcher takes separate random samples of $\maroonE{250}$ cars made in each year...."

Year | Population proportion | Sample size
:-: | :-: | :-:
2006 | $\blueE{p_{2006}}=\blueE{0.95}$ | $\maroonE{n_{2006}}=\maroonE{250}$
2017 | $\purpleD{p_{2017}}=\purpleD{0.72}$ | $\goldE{n_{2017}}=\goldE{250}$

assessment_items.question_content:
A quality control manager takes an SRS of $500$ phone batteries from factory A, where $5\%$ of the batteries have a defect. The manager also takes an independent SRS of $250$ batteries from factory B, where $9\%$  of the batteries have a defect. The manager will then look at the difference $(\text{B}-\text{A})$ between the proportions of defective batteries in each sample.

**What are the mean and standard deviation of the sampling distribution of the difference in sample proportions?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Formulas

The mean of the sampling distribution of $\hat{p}_1-\hat{p}_2$ is the difference of the population proportions:

$\mu_{\hat{p}_1-\hat{p}_2}=p_1-p_2$

The standard deviation of $\hat{p}_1-\hat{p}_2$ is: 

$\sigma_{\hat{p}_1-\hat{p}_2}=\sqrt{\dfrac{p_1\left(1-p_1\right)}{n_1}+\dfrac{p_2\left(1-p_2\right)}{n_2}}$

(where $n_1$ and $n_2$ are the sizes of each sample).

###Identifying values

"...A quality control manager takes an SRS of $\maroonE{500}$ phone batteries from factory A, where $\blueE{5\%}$ of the batteries have a defect. The manager also takes an independent SRS of $\goldE{250}$ batteries from factory B, where $\purpleD{9\%}$  of the batteries have a defect...."

Factory | Population proportion | Sample size
:-: | :-: | :-:
A | $\blueE{p_\text{A}}=\blueE{0.05}$ | $\maroonE{n_\text{A}}=\maroonE{500}$
B | $\purpleD{p_\text{B}}=\purpleD{0.09}$ | $\goldE{n_\text{B}}=\goldE{250}$

###Answer

The mean and standard deviation of the sampling distribution of $\hat{p}_\text{B}-\hat{p}_\text{A}$ are:

$\begin{align}
\mu_{\hat{p}_\text{B}-\hat{p}_\text{A}}&=0.04
\\\\
\sigma_{\hat{p}_\text{B}-\hat{p}_\text{A}} &= \sqrt{\dfrac{0.05(0.95)}{500}+\dfrac{0.09(0.91)}{250}}
\end{align}$

assessment_items.question_content:
A roadside assistance company gets millions of requests per year, with about a quarter of the requests in summer and a quarter in winter. Suppose that $12\%$ of all of the summer requests and $15\%$ of the winter requests are for help with a lockout. A data analyst pulls separate random samples of $200$ summer and winter requests and will look at the difference $(\text{summer}-\text{winter})$ between the proportion of requests for help with a lockout in each sample. 

**What are the mean and standard deviation of the sampling distribution of the difference in sample proportions?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Formulas

The mean of the sampling distribution of $\hat{p}_1-\hat{p}_2$ is the difference of the population proportions:

$\mu_{\hat{p}_1-\hat{p}_2}=p_1-p_2$

The standard deviation of $\hat{p}_1-\hat{p}_2$ is: 

$\sigma_{\hat{p}_1-\hat{p}_2}=\sqrt{\dfrac{p_1\left(1-p_1\right)}{n_1}+\dfrac{p_2\left(1-p_2\right)}{n_2}}$

(where $n_1$ and $n_2$ are the sizes of each sample).

###Identifying values

"...A data analyst pulls separate random samples of $\maroonE{200}$ summer and winter requests....$\blueE{12\%}$ of all of the summer requests and $\purpleD{15\%}$ of the winter requests are for help with a lockout...."

Season | Population proportion | Sample size
:-: | :-: | :-:
Summer | $\blueE{p_\text{S}}=\blueE{0.12}$ | $\maroonE{n_\text{S}}=\maroonE{200}$
Winter | $\purpleD{p_\text{W}}=\purpleD{0.15}$ | $\goldE{n_\text{W}}=\goldE{200}$

###Answer

The mean and standard deviation of the sampling distribution of $\hat{p}_\text{S}-\hat{p}_\text{W}$ are:

$\begin{align}
\mu_{\hat{p}_\text{S}-\hat{p}_\text{W}}&=-0.03
\\\\
\sigma_{\hat{p}_\text{S}-\hat{p}_\text{W}} &= \sqrt{\dfrac{0.12(0.88)}{200}+\dfrac{0.15(0.85)}{200}}
\end{align}$

assessment_items.question_content:
A newspaper editor wants to investigate whether the residents of two counties support the proposal to build a wind farm. She plans to take an SRS of $30$ residents from the southern county and $40$ residents from the northern county, then look at the difference $(\text{southern}-\text{northern})$ between the proportions of residents that support the proposal in each sample. Suppose that $65\%$ of the residents of the southern county and $52\%$ of the residents of the northern county approve.

**What are the mean and standard deviation of the sampling distribution of the difference in sample proportions?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Formulas

The mean of the sampling distribution of $\hat{p}_1-\hat{p}_2$ is the difference of the population proportions:

$\mu_{\hat{p}_1-\hat{p}_2}=p_1-p_2$

The standard deviation of $\hat{p}_1-\hat{p}_2$ is: 

$\sigma_{\hat{p}_1-\hat{p}_2}=\sqrt{\dfrac{p_1\left(1-p_1\right)}{n_1}+\dfrac{p_2\left(1-p_2\right)}{n_2}}$

(where $n_1$ and $n_2$ are the sizes of each sample).

###Identifying values

"...She plans to take an SRS of $\maroonE{30}$ residents from the southern county and $\goldE{40}$ residents from the northern county....$\blueE{65\%}$ of the residents of the southern county and $\purpleD{52\%}$ of the residents of the northern county approve...."

County | Population proportion | Sample size
:-: | :-: | :-:
Southern | $\blueE{p_\text{S}}=\blueE{0.65}$ | $\maroonE{n_\text{S}}=\maroonE{30}$
Northern | $\purpleD{p_\text{N}}=\purpleD{0.52}$ | $\goldE{n_\text{N}}=\goldE{40}$

###Answer

The mean and standard deviation of the sampling distribution of $\hat{p}_\text{S}-\hat{p}_\text{N}$ are:

$\begin{align}
\mu_{\hat{p}_\text{S}-\hat{p}_\text{N}}&=0.13
\\\\
\sigma_{\hat{p}_\text{S}-\hat{p}_\text{N}} &= \sqrt{\dfrac{0.65(0.35)}{30}+\dfrac{0.52(0.48)}{40}}
\end{align}$

assessment_items.question_content:
An educational researcher is studying two similar high schools in different states. Suppose that $50\%$ of all $425$ students at high school A have taken a college-level course, while $40\%$ of all $525$ students from high school B have taken such a course. The researcher plans on taking separate random samples of $50$ students from each high school to look at the difference $(\text{A}-\text{B})$ between the proportions of students who have taken a college-level course in each sample.

Consider the formula:  

$\sigma_{\hat{p}_\text{A}-\hat{p}_\text{B}}=\sqrt{\dfrac{p_\text{A}\left(1-p_\text{A}\right)}{n_\text{A}}+\dfrac{p_\text{B}\left(1-p_\text{B}\right)}{n_\text{B}}}$

**Why is it not appropriate for the researcher to use this formula for the standard deviation of $\hat{p}_\text{A}-\hat{p}_\text{B}$?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Independence conditions

To use this formula for $\sigma_{\hat{p}_\text{A}-\hat{p}_\text{B}}$, we need two types of independence:

1. **Independent samples**: This formula relies on adding variances of $\hat{p}_\text{A}$ and $\hat{p}_\text{B}$, and we learned that we can only add variances when we have independent random variables. In other words, knowledge of one sample shouldn't give us extra information about the other sample.
2. **Independent observations within each sample**: Each observation in a sample shouldn't give us extra information about the next. If we're sampling without replacement, and we're sampling less than $10\%$ of the population, we can treat  members in our sample as independent from each other. We call this the $10\%$ condition.

In this setting, are the samples independent? Are the members in each sample independent?

###Independent samples

The schools are in different states, and the researcher is taking separate random samples, so results from one sample wouldn't give us extra information about the other sample.

It's reasonable to treat these as independent samples.

###Independence within high school A sample

The researcher wants to sample $50$ students from high school A. To pass the $10\%$ condition there would need to be at least $500$ students in that population, but there are only $425$ students at high school A.

So we **cannot** treat students in this sample as being independent. The researcher would need to take a smaller sample, or better yet, use a different formula that is beyond the scope of this course.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

###Answer

It is not appropriate for the researcher to use this standard deviation formula because we cannot assume independence for the students sampled from high school A.

choices.content: The samples are not independent of each other.
We cannot assume independence for the students sampled from high school A.
We cannot assume independence for the students sampled from high school B.

assessment_items.question_content:
A security system company wants to know whether there are differences in the time of day of residential and commercial burglaries. They read the records from a randomly selected sample of $60$ of the $830$ residential burglaries and $60$ of the $447$ commercial burglaries in the past year from their town database.

Suppose that $65\%$ of the residential burglaries and $31\%$ of the commercial burglaries happened during the day. The company plans to look at the difference $(\hat p_\text{R} - \hat p_\text{C})$ between the proportions of daytime burglaries in each sample.

Consider the formula:  

$\sigma_{\hat{p}_\text{A}-\hat{p}_\text{B}}=\sqrt{\dfrac{p_\text{A}\left(1-p_\text{A}\right)}{n_\text{A}}+\dfrac{p_\text{B}\left(1-p_\text{B}\right)}{n_\text{B}}}$

**Why is it not appropriate for the company to use this formula for the standard deviation of $\hat p_\text{R} - \hat p_\text{C}$?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Independence conditions

To use this formula for $\sigma_{\hat{p}_\text{A}-\hat{p}_\text{B}}$, we need two types of independence:

1. **Independent samples**: This formula relies on adding variances of $\hat{p}_\text{A}$ and $\hat{p}_\text{B}$, and we learned that we can only add variances when we have independent random variables. In other words, knowledge of one sample shouldn't give us extra information about the other sample.
2. **Independent observations within each sample**: Each observation in a sample shouldn't give us extra information about the next. If we're sampling without replacement, and we're sampling less than $10\%$ of the population, we can treat  members in our sample as independent from each other. We call this the $10\%$ condition.

In this setting, are the samples independent? Are the members in each sample independent?

###Independent samples

Residential and commercial burglaries are mutually exclusive, and the company is taking separate random samples, so results from one sample wouldn't give us extra information about the other sample.

It's reasonable to treat these as independent samples.

###Independence within the residential sample

The company wants to sample $60$ burglaries from the residential burglaries. To pass the $10\%$ condition there would need to be at least $600$ burglaries in that population, and there are actually $830$.

So we can treat burglaries in this sample as being independent.

###Independence within the commercial sample

The company wants to sample $60$ burglaries from the commercial burglaries. To pass the $10\%$ condition there would need to be at least $600$ burglaries in that population, but there are only $447$ commercial burglaries.

So we **cannot** treat burglaries in this sample as being independent. The company would need to take a smaller sample, or better yet, use a different formula that is beyond the scope of this course.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

###Answer

It is not appropriate for the company to use this standard deviation formula because we cannot assume independence for the burglaries sampled from commercial burglaries.

choices.content: The samples are not independent of each other.
We cannot assume independence for the burglaries sampled from residential burglaries.
We cannot assume independence for the burglaries sampled from commercial burglaries.

assessment_items.question_content:
Ryker has a shipment of $420$ regular balloons and a shipment of $80$ confetti balloons to decorate a banquet hall. He suspects that the confetti balloons pop more often than the regular balloons do, so he takes a random sample of $20$ of each kind of balloon, inflates them, looks at the difference $(\text{confetti}-\text{regular})$ between the proportions of balloons that pop from each shipment.

Consider the formula:  

$\sigma_{\hat{p}_\text{A}-\hat{p}_\text{B}}=\sqrt{\dfrac{p_\text{A}\left(1-p_\text{A}\right)}{n_\text{A}}+\dfrac{p_\text{B}\left(1-p_\text{B}\right)}{n_\text{B}}}$

**Why is it not appropriate for Ryker to use this formula for the standard deviation of $\hat{p}_\text{C}-\hat{p}_\text{R}$?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Independence conditions

To use this formula for $\sigma_{\hat{p}_\text{A}-\hat{p}_\text{B}}$, we need two types of independence:

1. **Independent samples**: This formula relies on adding variances of $\hat{p}_\text{A}$ and $\hat{p}_\text{B}$, and we learned that we can only add variances when we have independent random variables. In other words, knowledge of one sample shouldn't give us extra information about the other sample.
2. **Independent observations within each sample**: Each observation in a sample shouldn't give us extra information about the next. If we're sampling without replacement, and we're sampling less than $10\%$ of the population, we can treat  members in our sample as independent from each other. We call this the $10\%$ condition.

In this setting, are the samples independent? Are the members in each sample independent?

###Independent samples

The balloons are from different shipments, and Ryker is taking separate random samples, so results from one sample wouldn't give us extra information about the other sample.

It's reasonable to treat these as independent samples.

###Independence within the confetti balloon sample

Ryker wants to sample $20$ balloons from the confetti balloon shipment. To pass the $10\%$ condition there would need to be at least $200$ balloons in that population, but there are only $80$ confetti balloons.

So we **cannot** treat the balloons in this sample as being independent. Ryker would need to take a smaller sample, or better yet, use a different formula that is beyond the scope of this course.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

###Answer

It is not appropriate for Ryker to use this standard deviation formula because we cannot assume independence for the balloons sampled from the confetti balloons.

choices.content: The samples are not independent of each other.
We cannot assume independence for the balloons sampled from the confetti balloons.
We cannot assume independence for the balloons sampled from the regular balloons.

assessment_items.question_content:
All freshmen at Success University take an orientation class. The professors plan on taking an SRS of $70$ of the $800$ freshmen and asking them twice whether they agree with the statement, "If I struggle with my homework, I can find help," once before the class and once after. They plan to look at the difference $(\text{after} - \text{before})$ between the proportions of freshmen who agree with the statement. Suppose that before the class, $30\%$ of the freshmen agree, while after the class, $85\%$ agree.

Consider the formula:  

$\sigma_{\hat{p}_\text{A}-\hat{p}_\text{B}}=\sqrt{\dfrac{p_\text{A}\left(1-p_\text{A}\right)}{n_\text{A}}+\dfrac{p_\text{B}\left(1-p_\text{B}\right)}{n_\text{B}}}$

**Why is it not appropriate for the professors to use this formula for the standard deviation of the distribution of the difference in sample proportions?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Independence conditions

To use this formula for $\sigma_{\hat{p}_\text{A}-\hat{p}_\text{B}}$, we need two types of independence:

1. **Independent samples**: This formula relies on adding variances of $\hat{p}_\text{A}$ and $\hat{p}_\text{B}$, and we learned that we can only add variances when we have independent random variables. In other words, knowledge of one sample shouldn't give us extra information about the other sample.
2. **Independent observations within each sample**: Each observation in a sample shouldn't give us extra information about the next. If we're sampling without replacement, and we're sampling less than $10\%$ of the population, we can treat  members in our sample as independent from each other. We call this the $10\%$ condition.

In this setting, are the samples independent? Are the members in each sample independent?

###Independent samples

The professors are taking a single sample, then asking the same freshmen the question twice. A student who already knew how to find help before the class is likely to say that they know how to find help after the class.

So we **cannot** treat the samples as independent of each other.

###Answer

It is not appropriate for the professors to use this standard deviation formula because the samples are not independent of each other.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

choices.content: The samples are not independent of each other.
We cannot assume independence for the students sampled before taking the class.
We cannot assume independence for the students sampled after taking the class.

assessment_items.question_content:
Amy wants to find the difference $\hat p_\text{Green} - \hat p_\text{Pink}$ in the proportions of green and pink balloons in packages of assorted balloons. Each package has $100$ balloons in $6$ colors. She randomly selects a package, calculates the proportion of green balloons and the proportion of pink balloons in the package, then finds the difference between the proportions. She repeats this process until she has selected $50$ packages to build a sampling distribution.

Consider the formula:  

$\sigma_{\hat{p}_\text{A}-\hat{p}_\text{B}}=\sqrt{\dfrac{p_\text{A}\left(1-p_\text{A}\right)}{n_\text{A}}+\dfrac{p_\text{B}\left(1-p_\text{B}\right)}{n_\text{B}}}$

**Why is it not appropriate for Amy to use this formula for the standard deviation of $\hat{p}_\text{Green}-\hat{p}_\text{Pink}$?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Independence conditions

To use this formula for $\sigma_{\hat{p}_\text{A}-\hat{p}_\text{B}}$, we need two types of independence:

1. **Independent samples**: This formula relies on adding variances of $\hat{p}_\text{A}$ and $\hat{p}_\text{B}$, and we learned that we can only add variances when we have independent random variables. In other words, knowledge of one sample shouldn't give us extra information about the other sample.
2. **Independent observations within each sample**: Each observation in a sample shouldn't give us extra information about the next. If we're sampling without replacement, and we're sampling less than $10\%$ of the population, we can treat  members in our sample as independent from each other. We call this the $10\%$ condition.

In this setting, are the samples independent? Are the members in each sample independent?

###Independent samples

Each time, Amy is taking a single sample, then checking the colors of the balloons twice. A package with a relatively higher proportion of one color is likely to have relatively lower proportion of the other color.

So we **cannot** treat the samples as independent of each other.

###Answer

It is not appropriate for Amy to use this standard deviation formula because the samples are not independent of each other.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

choices.content: The samples are not independent of each other.
We cannot assume independence for the packages sampled from which Amy calculated the proportion of pink balloons.
We cannot assume independence for the packages sampled from which Amy calculated the proportion of green balloons.

assessment_items.question_content:
Suppose that $55\%$ of the approximately $4{,}000$ school-aged children in the county have vitamin deficiency. A nutritionist takes a cluster random sample of $100$ of those children. They test the children for vitamin deficiency. They provide the children with vitamin-rich snacks at school for three months, then retest them. They plan to look at the difference $(\text{after} - \text{before})$ between the proportions of children with vitamin deficiency. 

Consider the formula:  

$\sigma_{\hat{p}_\text{A}-\hat{p}_\text{B}}=\sqrt{\dfrac{p_\text{A}\left(1-p_\text{A}\right)}{n_\text{A}}+\dfrac{p_\text{B}\left(1-p_\text{B}\right)}{n_\text{B}}}$

**Why is it not appropriate for the nutritionist to use this formula for the standard deviation of $\hat{p}_\text{A}-\hat{p}_\text{B}$?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Independence conditions

To use this formula for $\sigma_{\hat{p}_\text{A}-\hat{p}_\text{B}}$, we need two types of independence:

1. **Independent samples**: This formula relies on adding variances of $\hat{p}_\text{A}$ and $\hat{p}_\text{B}$, and we learned that we can only add variances when we have independent random variables. In other words, knowledge of one sample shouldn't give us extra information about the other sample.
2. **Independent observations within each sample**: Each observation in a sample shouldn't give us extra information about the next. If we're sampling without replacement, and we're sampling less than $10\%$ of the population, we can treat  members in our sample as independent from each other. We call this the $10\%$ condition.

In this setting, are the samples independent? Are the members in each sample independent?

###Independent samples

The nutritionist is taking a single sample, then testing the same children twice. A student who did not have vitamin deficiency before getting the snacks is likely to still not have vitamin deficiency after getting the snacks.

So we **cannot** treat the samples as independent of each other.

###Answer

It is not appropriate for the nutritionist to use this standard deviation formula because the samples are not independent of each other.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

choices.content: The samples are not independent of each other.
We cannot assume independence for the students sampled before receiving the snacks.
We cannot assume independence for the students sampled after receiving the snacks.
